# Transcrição: 03-Mapping Emotions

- **Fonte:** 03-Mapping Emotions
- **Duração:** 05:58
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

The first and easiest way to get your audience to connect with your story emotionally is to map out the emotions of your story. Essentially it is this. In the best version of storytelling, the most idealized version of storytelling, your audience is going to feel the things that you felt over the course of the story. So that means if you are sad in your story, your audience should be sad also for you or perhaps just experiencing the overall sense of sadness. When you are surprised in your story, your audience should also be surprised. And when you're happy, your audience should be happy. Essentially, the best version of every story is an emotional journey where the audience feels the things that you felt at the moment that you're describing them. Now that's extremely difficult to do. That is a high bar that is rarely achieved completely but can be achieved at least to some degree. The first thing you have to do is to map out the emotions of the story, which is to say you have to know how you felt so you can get the audience to feel the same way. So I want you to take that comic book pain, that physical manifestation of your story, and we're going to map out the emotions of every scene. Think about the story that I just told you. What emotions did I experience? And what emotions did I want you to experience? The beginning of my story, I'm in an apartment and I'm very sad. I want you to either sense my sadness or maybe even feel sad for me. The way I did that was described how sad I was. I gave you indicators about my sadness. I talked about how I was forgetting to sleep, how I wasn't changing the sheets in my bed, how I didn't have a shower curtain. I talked about how my apartment smelled like something that I didn't like. All of those things were there to convince you how sad and how pathetic I was feeling. I opened the story with water over raisin bran, which was at the time the saddest thing I could remember doing. Did you feel sad for me? Did you feel a general malaise perhaps? I hope you did. That was the goal. And then the story shifted. It shifted to my wife. I wanted you to hear my wife and be a little upset with her. I wanted you to hear her talking to me and be both confused and annoyed. Confused, I want space, but I don't know what that means. And annoyed. How dare you call me boring? I'm a wedding DJ. Someone named their dog after me because I'm fun. Did you get annoyed with my ex-wife? I was hoping you would. I wanted you to be a little bit on my side.

## [03:00 - 05:58]

I wanted you to be upset for me. Were you? That was the goal. That was the emotional moment of that scene. And then the final scene outside the apartment. The Indians are moving out. A new family is moving in. No more curry. I want you to feel a little bit of joy. I remember in that moment seeing those wonderful people move out with all of their curry and being ecstatic thinking, finally something good has happened to me. I wanted you to feel a little bit of joy for me. And then I wanted you to experience that final moment in the same way I experienced it. The appearance of the bride and the best man. I wanted you to feel that realization the way I did. I wanted you to put it together before I put it together in words. So you could feel that stone in your heart knowing my God, the woman ran away with the best man. And then when I was sitting at that table, the same place the story began, I ended in the same place. I wanted you to feel hope. I wanted you to feel like maybe it's not so bad after all. I wanted you to feel the contrast between the beginning and the end. I once was a guy who sat at a kitchen table and was deeply sad about his life. And then some stuff happened and I became a guy who was slightly hopeful about the rest of his life. I wanted you to follow that emotional journey with me. The problem or the mistake that storytellers make is they never take any of these things into consideration. They don't think about the idea that I want my audience to feel what I was feeling to some degree. I want them to feel an approximation of what I was feeling over the course of the story. And the first and most important way to do it is to actually identify those feelings. Go through your story and identify each one of those moments and ask yourself, how was I feeling? And then how can I convince my audience to feel the same way? I often describe stories as arguments. We're making an argument to our audience. I'm arguing that I'm sad. I want to argue so strongly that I'm sad that you kind of feel sad for me too. I want to argue that my wife is not really being good to me, that she might be wrong, that I might be the good guy in this story. I need to argue with you that I might be right and she might be wrong. And then I want to argue that maybe there's a little joy at the end, that there's hope. That's how we have to think about stories. But we can't do any of it if we don't map out that emotional journey. When you're crafting stories, you have to know how you felt so you know how the audience should feel.
